literal: rule, method, logical argument; from ni + √ī, "that into which [reasoning] leads"
invariant_definition: Nyāya is one of the six orthodox (āstika) darśanas, the school of logic, epistemology, and debate founded on Gautama (Akṣapāda)'s Nyāya-Sūtra (c. 2nd c. CE) with Vātsyāyana's Bhāṣya. Its enduring contribution is a systematic theory of the means of valid knowledge (pramāṇa) — recognizing perception, inference, comparison, and testimony — and a rigorous account of inference (anumāna) through the five-membered syllogism. Ontologically a pluralist realism (closely allied with Vaiśeṣika as the Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika synthesis), it argues for an enduring self, a creator Īśvara, and liberation as release from suffering attained through correct knowledge of reality.
